caption:
JavaScript try/catch is lying to you.

Rust's Result type forces you to handle EVERY error. No silent failures. No forgotten catch blocks. The compiler won't let you ship broken error handling.

Which pattern would you trust? A or B?

#rust #javascript #errorhandling #coding #programming #developer #rustlang #cleancode #typesafe #codingtips

slides.title: Which error handling is safer?
slides.problem: Handle a function that might fail

a.label: JS try/catch

b.label: Rust Result

slides.title: B wins. Errors are values, not surprises.

verdicts.title: Catch-all hides the real problem
verdicts.text: The catch block gets 'any'. You don't know which line failed or what type the error is. Easy to forget. Silent failures everywhere.

verdicts.title: The compiler forces you to handle it
verdicts.text: Result<T, E> makes errors part of the type system. The ? operator propagates them cleanly. You literally cannot compile code with unhandled errors.

slides.takeaway: In Rust, 'it compiles' means every error path is handled.

slides.title: The ? operator is magic.
slides.sub: Propagate errors in one character. Type-safe all the way up.

items.text: Result<T, E> — success OR error, never both
? operator — propagate errors in one char
match on Result — handle Ok and Err explicitly
No exceptions — errors are values, not surprises

slides.teaser: Next: Claude Code + MCP — give AI your entire stack
